title: What is family violence? - Canada.ca
content:
## What is family violence?
If you’re in immediate danger or need urgent medical  support, call 9-1-1.
If you or  someone you know needs support, you can access family violence helplines and services  across Canada .
Learn about the types of family violence, the forms it takes,  its health and social impacts, and how to get help.
- Defining family violence
- Types of family violence
- What family violence looks like
- Health and social impacts of family violence

## Defining  family violence
Family violence is when  someone in a family or close relationship uses behaviour that’s violent,  threatening, neglectful or controlling. The behaviour makes another person feel  unsafe or afraid.
Family violence can happen:
- regardless of age, education or where you live
- in many kinds of relationships and family  structures
It  also affects people of all races, religions, gender identities and sexual  orientations. When violence is committed against someone based on their gender,  gender identity, gender expression or perceived gender, it’s also a form of  gender-based violence.
Learn  more about gender-based violence .

## Types  of family violence
Types of family  violence include:
- child maltreatment physical,  sexual, emotional abuse and neglect exposure  to intimate partner violence seeing,  hearing or being indirectly exposed to abuse and injuries between adults in the  home experiencing  the outcome, like damaged property or possessions
- physical,  sexual, emotional abuse and neglect
- exposure  to intimate partner violence seeing,  hearing or being indirectly exposed to abuse and injuries between adults in the  home experiencing  the outcome, like damaged property or possessions
- seeing,  hearing or being indirectly exposed to abuse and injuries between adults in the  home
- experiencing  the outcome, like damaged property or possessions
- intimate partner violence physical,  sexual, psychological, emotional, economic abuse in a marriage, common-law or  dating relationship, even after it has ended
- physical,  sexual, psychological, emotional, economic abuse in a marriage, common-law or  dating relationship, even after it has ended
- mistreatment of older persons (also       known as elder abuse) physical,  psychological, financial or sexual abuse or neglect by an intimate partner,  child, grandchild, caregiver or others where there’s an expectation of trust
- physical,  psychological, financial or sexual abuse or neglect by an intimate partner,  child, grandchild, caregiver or others where there’s an expectation of trust
Family violence  also includes:
- harmful practices underage  or forced marriage female  genital mutilation or cutting violence  committed in the name of “honour”
- underage  or forced marriage
- female  genital mutilation or cutting
- violence  committed in the name of “honour”
- sibling abuse physical,  emotional, sexual abuse of a sibling by another includes  siblings by birth, step-siblings, foster siblings, adopted siblings
- physical,  emotional, sexual abuse of a sibling by another includes  siblings by birth, step-siblings, foster siblings, adopted siblings
- includes  siblings by birth, step-siblings, foster siblings, adopted siblings
- child-to-parent violence and aggression a  pattern of violent and aggressive behaviour in children and youth towards their  parents or main caregivers includes  physical, psychological, emotional and financial abuse
- a  pattern of violent and aggressive behaviour in children and youth towards their  parents or main caregivers includes  physical, psychological, emotional and financial abuse
- includes  physical, psychological, emotional and financial abuse
Access information on sibling violence.

## What  family violence looks like
The content in this  section talks about experiences of family violence. This may be hard to read,  and it may bring up strong feelings, especially for survivors and their loved  ones. Please take care.
If you need  support, you can visit our Mental health supports page or our Family violence helplines and services page.
Family violence can  take many forms:
- emotional or psychological abuse
- harassment and stalking
- threats or harm to pets or property
Physical abuse includes:
- locking someone in a room
Sexual abuse is  an act of a sexual nature that occurs without consent. It can occur with or  without physical contact and with or without violence.
Canada has laws about  consent:
- The age  of consent is 16 years. This means that a person must be at least 16 years old  to legally agree (consent) to sexual activity.
- In some  cases, the age of consent is higher (for example, when there is a relationship  of trust, authority or dependency).
All sexual activity  without consent is a criminal offence, regardless of age.
Learn  about the laws on age of consent for sexual activity.
Sexual abuse  includes:
- non-consensual sexual touching, kissing or penetration of any kind       with an object or any part of the body
- forcing a person to touch themselves or someone else for sexual       purposes
- exposing a person to one’s genitals without consent (called       exhibitionism)
- engaging in online sexual violence, such as: online child sexual exploitation threatening  to distribute sexual images or information (called sextortion) taking or distributing sexual images, videos  or “deep fakes”
- online child sexual exploitation
- threatening  to distribute sexual images or information (called sextortion)
- taking or distributing sexual images, videos  or “deep fakes”

### Emotional or psychological abuse
Emotional or psychological  abuse involves words or actions that harm or frighten someone or weaken their  self-respect.
Emotional or  psychological abuse includes:
- making  someone feel worthless or afraid
- threatening to tell others       about someone’s sexual orientation or gender identity without consent
Neglect is when a  caregiver (parent, guardian or person in a position of trust) doesn’t provide:
- basic  needs such as food, warm clothing, shelter, medical care, supervision and  hygiene or
- the  emotional support that a child or dependent adult needs to feel safe and secure
It’s an ongoing  pattern rather than a single incident.
Economic abuse includes:
- taking or controlling someone’s money
- using money or property to control, exploit or harm someone
- limiting a person’s independence, such as not allowing them to work       or putting debt in their name
Coercive control is  a pattern of behaviour that someone uses to limit their partner’s sense of  control and freedom. This behaviour can make the partner fear for their own  safety or the safety of people they know. Over time, the behaviour causes the partner  to feel stuck and unable to act independently or safely.
Coercive control can  include behaviours that are against the law, such as sexual or physical  violence.
It can also include less obvious actions, such as:
- threatening their       immigration status
- threatening  to take away their children
- restricting  their opinions or gender expression
- blocking  their access to health care or medication
- monitoring  or limiting where they go and who they see
- harming  or threatening to harm themselves or someone the partner knows
Learn more about coercive control.

### Harassment  and stalking
Harassment and stalking involve repeated unwanted contact, following,  spying on, threatening, intimidating or making someone fear for their safety.
Harassment and stalking can also take place  online, through:
- social  media and email or
- software  programs, apps and devices that monitor another person’s activity, often  without their knowledge

### Threats  or harm to pets and property
Hurting animals and  breaking or threatening to destroy things someone cares about is a form of abuse.

## Health and social impacts of family  violence
Family violence can harm a person’s physical, mental and  emotional health. The impacts can be complex and long-lasting, even affecting  future generations. They can also affect a person’s social or economic  situation.
Experiencing family violence increases  the risk of:
- fear, shame or isolation
- alcohol or drug use as a way to cope
- anxiety, depression or post-traumatic       stress (PTSD), self-injury, suicidal ideation or suicide attempts
- bruises, broken bones or brain injuries
- trouble at school or work and problems       with relationships
- chronic pain, headaches, stomach       problems or sleep issues
- intergenerational trauma Children who grow up with family violence are at  greater risk of experiencing or using violence later in life.
- Children who grow up with family violence are at  greater risk of experiencing or using violence later in life.
Longer-lasting or more severe abuse can lead to greater  health and social impacts and to longer recovery time for individuals and  families. For example, a child who lives with abuse or is exposed to violence  in the home can experience health impacts throughout their life.
If you’re experiencing family  violence, you don’t have to face it alone. You deserve to feel safe in your  home and in your relationships.
It’s natural to feel  unsure, afraid or confused about what to do next. It isn’t easy to address and  heal from trauma. Learning about family violence and its impact on you, your  children or other loved ones is an important step towards healing.
Consider talking to someone you trust:
- community       leader
- health       professional
You can also contact a local community organization, shelter  or crisis centre for guidance.
- Family       violence laws
- How to recognize       family violence
- How to respond to family violence
- Family       violence: Plan for your safety
- Get       help: Family violence helplines and services
- Canadian Centre for       Child Protection: Survivor and family support
